title: Topic 194: Why Players Create Tier Lists: The Compulsion to Rank Everything
content:
# Topic 194: Why Players Create Tier Lists: The Compulsion to Rank Everything

## ABSTRACT

In an increasingly complex world, humanity has once again found solace in the most primal of instincts: putting things in order. This paper delves into the modern manifestation of this compulsion, the ubiquitous “tier list.” From the hallowed halls of professional gaming to the mundane arguments of which breakfast cereal is “S-Tier,” the tier list has become the de facto tool for arbitrary yet passionate ranking. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, examining how social comparison theory, cognitive biases, and a desperate need for control in a chaotic world converge to create the perfect storm of hierarchical organization. Through a rigorous, albeit tongue-in-cheek, analysis of gaming communities and consumer behavior, we reveal that the desire to rank everything is not just a quirky hobby but a fundamental aspect of the human condition. Or, at the very least, a really good way to start an argument on the internet.

## INTRODUCTION

The internet, a realm once hailed as the great equalizer, has ironically become obsessed with establishing hierarchies. In the digital agora of forums, social media, and streaming platforms, a new form of philosophical discourse has emerged, not from the mouths of bearded Athenian scholars, but from the keyboards of gamers, anime enthusiasts, and fast-food connoisseurs. The subject of their intense debate? The tier list. This seemingly simple tool, a colorful grid of letters and images, has become the modern-day Rosetta Stone for understanding value, power, and, most importantly, who would win in a fight between a grilled cheese sandwich and a taco.

This paper will embark on a perilous journey into the heart of this ranking obsession, focusing on its most prominent battleground: the world of video games. We will explore why players, after spending hundreds of hours mastering a game, feel an overwhelming compulsion to distill their complex experiences into a simple, color-coded chart. Is it a noble pursuit of objective truth? A cry for validation in a world that refuses to acknowledge the strategic superiority of the long-barreled shotgun? Or is it just a way to make friends and then immediately lose them by declaring their favorite character “D-Tier?”

We will dissect the psychological drivers behind this behavior, from the innate human need for categorization and control to the powerful influence of social comparison. We will examine how the “Rank Length Effect” and the allure of “Upward Identification” can turn a simple game into a cutthroat competition for imaginary prestige. And, in the end, we will ask the most important question of all: is there a tier list for tier lists? (Spoiler alert: yes, and this paper is undoubtedly S-Tier).

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has long been fascinated by the human need to categorize and rank. While they may not have used the term “S-Tier,” early psychologists and sociologists laid the groundwork for understanding why we are so obsessed with putting things in their proper place. Festinger’s (1954) social comparison theory, for example, is the OG of tier list psychology. He argued that we have an innate drive to evaluate ourselves, and in the absence of objective standards, we look to others. In the gaming world, this translates to a constant, nagging question: “Am I as good as that streamer who can beat the final boss with a dance pad?” The tier list, then, becomes a sacred text, a guide to self-evaluation in a world of virtual chaos [1].

More recent research has built upon this foundation, exploring the cognitive benefits of hierarchy. Zitek and Phillips (2020) argue that hierarchies are “easy to process” and provide a “sense of control” [2]. This is a profoundly comforting thought for anyone who has spent an entire weekend trying to understand the patch notes of a new game update. The tier list, with its simple, linear progression from “God Tier” to “Why Does This Even Exist? Tier,” offers a beautiful illusion of order in a world of constant change. It’s the intellectual equivalent of tidying up your sock drawer, but with more explosions and dragon-slaying.

Of course, no discussion of ranking would be complete without mentioning the work of Isaac (2024), who has explored the “psychological pull of rankings and top lists” [3]. His research on the “Rank Length Effect” and “Source Attribution” sheds light on the subtle ways in which our perception of value is manipulated. For example, a character ranked #10 on a list of 100 is perceived as more impressive than a character ranked #10 on a list of 10. This is why tier lists are so often presented with a flourish of authority, as if they were handed down from on high by the gaming gods themselves. The more official and comprehensive a tier list appears, the more we are inclined to believe it, even if it was created by a teenager in their basement who thinks that pineapple on pizza is a good idea.

Finally, we must consider the specific context of gaming. Esteves et al. (2021) have shown that “upward identification” and “downward contrast” are the most influential comparison elements in game continuance [4]. In other words, we are motivated to keep playing when we see players who are better than us (upward identification) and when we see players who are worse than us (downward contrast). The tier list is the perfect tool for facilitating both of these comparisons. It allows us to dream of one day mastering the S-Tier characters while simultaneously feeling smugly superior to anyone who dares to main a D-Tier character. It’s a vicious cycle of aspiration and schadenfreude, and it’s what keeps the gaming industry afloat.

## ANALYSIS

Now that we have established that the compulsion to rank is not merely a fleeting trend but a deeply ingrained psychological imperative, let us dissect the beast itself. What exactly happens in the gamer's brain when they meticulously drag and drop JPEGs into a color-coded grid? The process, we argue, is a beautiful symphony of cognitive biases, social maneuvering, and a desperate, primal scream into the void for a semblance of order.

First, consider the **Cognitive Ease of Categorization**. The human brain is a notorious gas guzzler, constantly seeking shortcuts to conserve precious mental energy. Hierarchies and categories are the cognitive equivalent of a cheat code. As Zitek and Phillips (2020) noted, they are simply “easy to process” [2]. A tier list takes the bewildering complexity of a modern video game—with its hundreds of characters, weapons, and abilities, all interacting in a dizzying ballet of emergent gameplay—and reduces it to a simple, digestible format. It’s a mental heuristic that allows players to make quick judgments without the tedious process of, you know, thinking. Why spend hours analyzing frame data and matchup charts when you can simply consult the sacred scrolls of the community-approved tier list? This isn’t laziness; it’s cognitive efficiency.

Second, we have the **Illusion of Control in a Random Universe**. Games, much like life, are often chaotic and unpredictable. A perfectly executed strategy can be undone by a sudden lag spike, a random critical hit, or a teammate who appears to be playing with their feet. The tier list offers a powerful antidote to this existential dread. By imposing a rigid, hierarchical structure onto the game, players can create an illusion of predictability and control. It’s a way of saying, “The universe may be a swirling vortex of meaninglessness, but I’ll be damned if I don’t know that the ‘Thunder-Fury, Blessed Blade of the Windseeker’ is unequivocally S-Tier.” This desire for control is a powerful motivator, especially in a world where so much feels beyond our grasp [2].

Third, and perhaps most importantly, is the role of **Social Identity and Belonging**. In the grand theater of online gaming, the tier list is not just a tool for analysis; it’s a social contract. To agree on a tier list is to belong to a tribe. It’s a shared language, a common set of beliefs that binds a community together. When a player argues passionately for the promotion of their favorite character to a higher tier, they are not just arguing about pixels on a screen; they are fighting for their own identity as a member of that character’s loyal fanbase. Conversely, to disagree with the community’s tier list is to risk excommunication, to be cast out into the wilderness of casual play. The pressure to conform is immense, and the tier list becomes a powerful tool for enforcing community norms and maintaining social cohesion. It’s the digital equivalent of a secret handshake, but with more spreadsheets and angry forum posts.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly appreciate the beautiful absurdity of the tier list phenomenon, we must venture into the wild and observe it in its natural habitat. The gaming world is rife with examples of communities that have elevated the tier list from a simple pastime to a high art form.

Consider the case of **Super Smash Bros. Melee**, a game released in 2001 that has been kept alive for over two decades by a fiercely dedicated community. The Melee tier list is not just a guide; it is a sacred text, a living document that has been debated, revised, and fought over for generations of players. The top tiers are dominated by a handful of characters, the “gods” of the game, who have been optimized to a degree that borders on the supernatural. To play a character outside of the top tiers is to make a statement, to declare oneself a maverick, a rebel, a person who is willing to sacrifice victory for the sake of individuality. The pressure to conform to the tier list is immense, and the community’s reaction to a low-tier character winning a major tournament is a sight to behold, a mixture of awe, disbelief, and a frantic rush to update the tier list.

Then there is the world of **League of Legends**, a game with a roster of over 160 characters, each with their own unique abilities and playstyles. The League of Legends tier list is a constantly shifting landscape, with characters rising and falling in the rankings with every new patch and update. The community’s obsession with the tier list is so intense that it has spawned an entire cottage industry of websites, YouTubers, and streamers who dedicate their lives to analyzing the meta and predicting which characters will be “broken” in the next patch. For many players, the game is not just about playing; it’s about playing the *right* way, and the tier list is the ultimate arbiter of what is right.

But the tier list phenomenon is not limited to the world of competitive gaming. It has seeped into every corner of popular culture, from the mundane to the absurd. There are tier lists for fast-food restaurants, for breakfast cereals, for candy bars, for animes, and even for the days of the week (Tuesday is universally considered C-Tier). This proliferation of tier lists speaks to a deeper human need, a desire to impose order and meaning on a world that often feels chaotic and meaningless. It’s a way of making sense of the world, one color-coded grid at a time.

## DISCUSSION

The implications of our findings extend far beyond the digital battlegrounds of video games. The compulsion to rank everything, so vividly demonstrated by the tier list phenomenon, offers a profound insight into the modern consumer's psyche. In an age of overwhelming choice, the tier list serves as a powerful tool for navigating the marketplace. Why spend hours researching the best brand of a toaster when the internet has already collectively decided which one is S-Tier? This reliance on community-driven rankings has a significant impact on consumer behavior, creating a feedback loop where the popular become more popular, and the obscure are relegated to the D-Tier of history.

This has not gone unnoticed by marketers, who have become adept at manipulating the psychological principles that underpin the tier list. The use of “Top 10” lists, “Editor’s Choice” awards, and other forms of ranking are all attempts to leverage our innate desire for hierarchy and social proof. The “Rank Length Effect” and “Source Attribution” are not just academic curiosities; they are powerful tools in the arsenal of the modern marketer. By understanding the psychology of ranking, we can become more discerning consumers, more aware of the subtle ways in which our choices are being influenced.

Furthermore, the tier list phenomenon highlights a fundamental tension in modern society: the desire for individual expression versus the need for social belonging. On the one hand, we are encouraged to be unique, to forge our own path, to play the D-Tier character. On the other hand, we are social creatures, driven by a deep-seated need to belong to a tribe, to conform to the norms of our chosen community. The tier list is a microcosm of this struggle, a place where individual preference and collective consensus collide. It is a testament to our ability to create meaning and order in a world that often seems to have neither.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, cosmic tier list of human endeavors, the creation of tier lists may not rank as highly as, say, curing diseases or composing symphonies. But it is, we argue, a uniquely human and surprisingly complex activity. It is a testament to our ingenuity, our creativity, and our unwavering determination to impose order on a chaotic universe, one color-coded grid at a time.

From the hallowed halls of academic psychology to the dimly lit basements of professional gamers, the compulsion to rank is a universal constant. It is a reflection of our deepest psychological needs: the need for control, the need for social connection, and the need to know, once and for all, whether a hot dog is a sandwich (it’s C-Tier, and that’s a hill we’re willing to die on).

So the next time you find yourself embroiled in a heated debate about the relative merits of different brands of bottled water, do not despair. You are not wasting your time. You are participating in a rich and time-honored tradition of human intellectual inquiry. You are, in your own small way, a philosopher, a scientist, and a cultural critic. And for that, we salute you. Now, if you’ll excuse us, we have a very important tier list to create about the best kinds of office chairs.

## REFERENCES

[1] Festinger, L. (1954). A theory of social comparison processes. *Human relations*, *7*(2), 117-140.

[2] Zitek, E. M., & Phillips, L. T. (2020). Ease and control: the cognitive benefits of hierarchy. *Current Opinion in Psychology*, *33*, 131-135.

[3] Isaac, M. S. (2024). The Psychological Pull of Rankings and Top Lists. *Psychology Today*. Retrieved from https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/all-things-numbered/202410/the-psychological-pull-of-rankings-and-top-lists

[4] Esteves, J., Valogianni, K., & Greenhill, A. (2021). Online social games: The effect of social comparison elements on continuance behaviour. *Information & Management*, *58*(4), 103452.
